sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Diana Scott, is a 100-year-old woman who presents with a new diagnosis of a heart murmur, which her previous doctor described as a "worrisome" condition.
She reports severe pains in her legs and has been taking pain pills for this issue.
She also mentions that she gets short of breath when walking too fast or too long, or when drinking a full glass of water without stopping.
She reports using a walker due to fatigue and has a history of surgeries.
She denies chest pain and has not noticed irregular heartbeats.
She mentions swelling in her ankles, which has been going on for 6 months.
She has lost weight gradually over the past year and denies fevers, chills, or other symptoms.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of surgeries and a family history of heart problems, including a son who had a heart attack and a brother who had a stroke due to diabetes.
She has never smoked and has no known allergies to medications.
Review of systems: The patient reports being overly tired due to medication and has difficulty walking due to leg pain.
Current medications:
Amlodipine, lisinopril, simvastatin 20mg (lipids), meclizine (for lightheadedness and dizziness), levothyroxine (for hypothyroidism).
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has a 4 out of 6 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient is a 100-year-old woman with a cardiac murmur, likely representing aortic stenosis, and symptoms possibly due to severe aortic stenosis, but with a relatively preserved S2, more consistent with moderate stenosis.
Problem list: Aortic stenosis; possible severe aortic stenosis; PVCS; tachycardia; hyperlipidemia; lightheadedness/dizziness.
Plan:
I prescribed the patient to undergo an echocardiogram to assess her overall cardiac structure and function.
I will also consider an ischemic evaluation or further assessment with a heart monitor depending on the results of the echocardiogram.
I will send the results and recommendations to her primary care physician (PCP) to discuss the possibility of stopping her statin medication.
I will also recommend that she continue taking her current medications as prescribed.
I will follow up with her in 3-4 weeks to discuss the results and next steps.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in 3-4 weeks to discuss the results of the echocardiogram and determine the next course of action.
